title: Emergency Guide: My Dog Ate Sugar-Free Gum (Xylitol Poisoning)
meta_desc: If your dog ate gum with xylitol, this is a life-threatening emergency. Learn the risks, symptoms, and immediate steps to save your pet.
content_html:
TL;DR: Xylitol is a highly toxic sugar substitute that causes life-threatening low blood sugar and liver failure in dogs, requiring immediate emergency veterinary intervention.

What is xylitol poisoning and why is it dangerous for dogs?

Xylitol is a common sugar substitute found in sugar-free gums, mints, and toothpastes. While perfectly safe for humans, it is extremely toxic to dogs. When ingested by a dog, xylitol causes a rapid, massive release of insulin, which leads to a life-threatening drop in blood sugar (hypoglycemia) and, in some cases, acute liver failure.

Is xylitol ingestion a medical emergency for my dog?

  • Urgency Level: High. This is a critical medical emergency as xylitol is one of the most dangerous substances a dog can consume.
  • Symptoms like vomiting, weakness, or seizures can appear in as little as 30 minutes.
  • Internal damage can occur even before symptoms are visible.
  • You must contact a veterinarian or an animal poison control center immediately.

How does a photo of the product packaging help my veterinarian during triage?

  • If you have the packaging, take a clear photo of the ingredients list and the brand name.
  • Different brands contain different concentrations of xylitol.
  • Providing this photo to your veterinarian allows them to calculate the exact toxic dose your dog may have received.
  • This information is vital for determining the intensity of the treatment required.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In dogs, xylitol ingestion stimulates a rapid, dose-dependent insulin release, potentially causing profound hypoglycemia at dosages exceeding 75-100 mg/kg. Some dogs ingesting xylitol at dosages >500 mg/kg may develop severe hepatic insufficiency or failure. Hypoglycemia can manifest within 30 minutes or be delayed up to 12-18 hours, presenting as vomiting, weakness, ataxia, depression, hypokalemia, seizures, or coma. Liver injury signs may appear 24-48 hours post-ingestion, though liver enzyme increases can be detected within 8-12 hours. Emesis should only be attempted under veterinary supervision in asymptomatic animals, and activated charcoal is not recommended as it does not bind xylitol effectively. Animals ingesting >75-100 mg/kg should be hospitalized for blood glucose monitoring every 1-2 hours for at least 12 hours, while those ingesting >500 mg/kg should also have baseline liver values measured and reevaluated every 24 hours for at least 72 hours. Hypoglycemia is managed with IV dextrose boluses and/or constant-rate infusions until normoglycemia is maintained without supplementation, potentially lasting >24 hours. For dogs ingesting xylitol at >500 mg/kg, dextrose administration and hepatoprotectants like N-acetylcysteine, S-adenosylmethionine, and silymarin should be considered even if normoglycemic.

Chapter: Toxicology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What are the signs of xylitol poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common symptoms include vomiting, followed by signs of low blood sugar such as lethargy, stumbling, collapse, and seizures.

faqs.question: How much xylitol is toxic to a dog?
faqs.answer: Even very small amounts are dangerous. As little as 0.1 grams per kilogram of body weight can cause hypoglycemia, while higher doses can cause liver failure.

faqs.question: Should I induce vomiting at home?
faqs.answer: Do not induce vomiting unless specifically instructed to do so by a veterinarian, as it can be dangerous depending on your dog's current neurological state.
